flashcards.front: Define Innate (Native) Immunity.
flashcards.back: The resistance an individual possesses by virtue of genetic and constitutional make-up; present from birth, non-specific, and lacks immunological memory [1, 2].

flashcards.front: What are the four characteristic features of Acquired (Adaptive) Immunity?
flashcards.back: 1. Antigenic specificity, 2. Diversity, 3. Immunologic memory, 4. Self/non-self recognition [3-6].

flashcards.front: What is the 'Prozone Phenomenon' in serology?
flashcards.back: A failure of visible reaction in a test due to an excess of antibody, which inhibits the formation of a lattice [7].

flashcards.front: Define 'Hapten'.
flashcards.back: A low-molecular-weight substance that is not immunogenic by itself but becomes so when coupled with a large carrier protein [8, 9].

flashcards.front: What is an 'Epitope'?
flashcards.back: The smallest unit of antigenicity (antigenic determinant) that reacts with a specific antibody or T-cell receptor [9, 10].

flashcards.front: How do Superantigens activate T-cells?
flashcards.back: They bind directly to the lateral aspect of the TCR beta chain and MHC Class II outside the peptide-binding groove, activating large numbers of T-cells irrespective of specificity [11, 12].

flashcards.front: Which Immunoglobulin class is the only one that crosses the placenta?
Which Immunoglobulin is known as the 'Millionaire Molecule' and why?
flashcards.back: IgM, because it has a high molecular weight (900,000 to 1,000,000 Daltons) and exists as a pentamer [15].

flashcards.front: Which antibody class is the main component of local/mucosal immunity?
flashcards.back: Secretory IgA [16, 17].

flashcards.front: Which Immunoglobulin mediates Type I Hypersensitivity (anaphylaxis)?
Identify the 'C3 convertase' of the Classical Complement Pathway.
Identify the 'C3 convertase' of the Alternative Complement Pathway.
flashcards.back: C3bBb [21].

flashcards.front: What components make up the Membrane Attack Complex (MAC)?
Define Type I Hypersensitivity.
flashcards.back: Immediate, IgE-mediated reaction involving mast cell degranulation and release of vasoactive amines like histamine [24-26].

flashcards.front: Define Type II Hypersensitivity.
flashcards.back: Cytotoxic reaction where IgG or IgM binds to cell-surface antigens, leading to cell lysis (e.g., Hemolytic disease of the newborn) [27-29].

flashcards.front: Define Type III Hypersensitivity.
flashcards.back: Immune complex-mediated reaction where Ag-Ab complexes deposit in tissues and activate complement (e.g., Serum sickness, Arthus reaction) [29-31].

flashcards.front: Define Type IV Hypersensitivity.
flashcards.back: Delayed-type hypersensitivity (DTH) mediated by sensitized T-cells (not antibodies), appearing after 24–48 hours (e.g., Tuberculin test) [32-34].

flashcards.front: What is 'DiGeorge Syndrome'?
flashcards.back: A congenital aplasia/hypoplasia of the thymus resulting in deficient cell-mediated immunity (CMI) [35, 36].

flashcards.front: What is 'Bruton's Disease'?
flashcards.back: X-linked agammaglobulinemia, a primary B-cell immunodeficiency seen only in males, characterized by a lack of B-cells and plasma cells [37, 38].

flashcards.front: Where are MHC Class I molecules found?
flashcards.back: On the surface of virtually all nucleated cells in the body [39, 40].

flashcards.front: Where are MHC Class II molecules primarily found?
flashcards.back: Only on professional antigen-presenting cells (APCs) like macrophages, dendritic cells, and B-cells [41, 42].

flashcards.front: What is the clinical significance of HLA-B27?
flashcards.back: It is strongly associated with the autoimmune condition Ankylosing Spondylitis [43].

flashcards.front: Which blood group is the 'Universal Donor' and why?
flashcards.back: Group O, because its red cells carry no A or B antigens and are not attacked by anti-A or anti-B antibodies [44, 45].

flashcards.front: Which blood group is the 'Universal Recipient'?
flashcards.back: Group AB, as these individuals possess neither anti-A nor anti-B antibodies in their serum [46].

flashcards.front: What is the 'Bombay (OH) Blood Group'?
flashcards.back: A rare phenotype where antigens A, B, and H are all absent from the red cells [47].

flashcards.front: What defines an 'Allograft'?
flashcards.back: A graft between two genetically non-identical members of the same species [48].

flashcards.front: Define 'Graft-versus-Host (GVH) Reaction'.
flashcards.back: A condition where immunocompetent T-cells in the graft attack the antigens of an immunologically compromised recipient [49, 50].

flashcards.front: What is the primary function of the Spleen in immunology?
flashcards.back: It serves as a filter for blood and a 'graveyard' for effete red blood cells; its immune function is primarily against bloodborne antigens [51].

flashcards.front: What is 'Opsonization'?
flashcards.back: A process where pathogens are coated with antibodies or complement (C3b) to facilitate their phagocytosis [52, 53].

flashcards.front: What are 'Toll-like Receptors (TLRs)'?
flashcards.back: Pattern recognition receptors (PRRs) that recognize unique molecular patterns (PAMPs) shared by pathogens [54, 55].

flashcards.front: What is the role of 'Plasma Cells'?
flashcards.back: They are the final stage of B-cell differentiation and serve as antibody-secreting factories [56, 57].

flashcards.front: Define 'Immunological Tolerance'.
flashcards.back: A state of specific non-reactivity to an antigen that would normally elicit an immune response [58].

flashcards.front: What is 'Molecular Mimicry'?
flashcards.back: An autoimmune mechanism where microbial epitopes are identical to self-antigens, leading to an attack on the host's own tissues [59, 60].

flashcards.front: What are 'Primary Lymphoid Organs'?
flashcards.back: The Thymus and Bone Marrow, where lymphocytes undergo maturation and education [61, 62].

flashcards.front: What is 'Herd Immunity'?
flashcards.back: The overall level of immunity in a community; if a large proportion (approx. 80%) is immune, the likelihood of an epidemic is reduced [63, 64].

flashcards.front: What is the 'Negative Phase' in active immunity?
flashcards.back: A temporary fall in measurable antibody levels immediately following a booster dose, as the new antigen combines with existing antibodies [65, 66].

flashcards.front: Which cell provides non-specific defense against virus-infected and tumor cells?
flashcards.back: Natural Killer (NK) cells [3, 67].

flashcards.front: What is the diagnostic marker for Systemic Lupus Erythematosus (SLE)?
flashcards.back: The LE cell phenomenon and high titres of anti-dsDNA antibodies [68, 69].

flashcards.front: What is 'Myasthenia Gravis' mechanism?
flashcards.back: Autoantibodies against acetylcholine receptors at the myoneural junction prevent muscle contraction [70].

flashcards.front: Which test is used to detect toxigenicity in Diphtheria bacilli?
flashcards.back: The Elek test (a double diffusion in two dimensions) [71].
